brief.title: <abbr title='Home Box Office'>HBO</abbr> Max
brief.scope: Contributed to the design system initiative for the HBO Max website, building reusable, scalable UI components used across the streaming platform to ensure visual and functional consistency for a large, multi-team engineering organization.
brief.problem: A fast-growing streaming platform needed a unified design language that multiple teams could implement consistently, while supporting rapid feature development, augmented reality experiences, and rendering across different frameworks and rendering strategies (SSR, CMS-driven pages).
key_challenges: Building a design system flexible enough to support both React-based components and CMS-driven pages (Drupal).
Ensuring accessibility and mobile-first standards across a large component library.
Supporting emerging experiences like Augmented Reality within a traditional web stack.
key_contributions: Built and documented reusable UI components in Storybook using React, MUI, and Styled Components.
Implemented forms and validation logic with React Hook Forms integrated into the broader design system.
Collaborated across NextJS, Django, and Drupal-driven surfaces to keep component behavior consistent regardless of the rendering layer.
Supported testing coverage (Jest, Enzyme) to guarantee design system reliability.
technical_focus: Design system architecture and component documentation (Storybook).
brief.suggested_diagrams: Design system component hierarchy. Component consumption across NextJS/Drupal/Django surfaces. Component library governance and versioning flow.
system_impact: Reduced UI inconsistency across a large streaming platform by centralizing shared components.
Improved development velocity for feature teams reusing design system elements.
Enabled more consistent accessibility compliance across the site.
learnings: Design systems are as much an organizational challenge as a technical one — success depends on adoption, documentation, and governance, not just component quality. This experience reinforced the importance of designing for consumers of a system, not just for the system itself.
